---
title: "![](images/icons/icon_14.png){.title-icon} Time-to-event"
subtitle: "Survival analysis: Kaplan-Meier, Cox regression, assumptions and competing risks"
date: last-modified
---

::: {.callout-warning}
**Under development.** This page shows minimal, verified examples. More detail is coming.
:::

Time-to-event analysis (survival analysis) looks at the **time until an event** and handles the fact that not everyone reaches the event during follow-up (censoring). It is the typical framework for a cohort study with an outcome over time.

::: {.callout-note}
The code examples use generic path and variable names. Adapt them to your project. The packages (`survival`, `survminer`, `tidycmprsk`) must be installed in your R environment on DST.
:::

---

## Starting point

You need two things per person: **follow-up time** and **event status** (both built in [Phase 11](11_udtraek-variable.qmd)/[Phase 12](12_data-manipulation.qmd)).

```r
library(survival)                          # Surv(), survfit(), coxph(), cox.zph()

df <- readRDS("path/to/analysis.rds")      # analysis-ready dataset, one row per person

# Surv() combines time + status into one "survival object" used by the models:
#   time_days = days from index to event OR censoring (whichever comes first)
#   outcome   = 1 if the event occurred, 0 if the person was censored
sobj <- Surv(time = df$time_days, event = df$outcome)
```

## Kaplan-Meier curves

`survfit()` computes the Kaplan-Meier estimate (the event-free proportion over time), and `ggsurvplot()` draws it nicely.

```r
library(survminer)                         # ggsurvplot()

fit <- survfit(Surv(time_days, outcome) ~ exposure,   # one curve per exposure group
               data = df)

ggsurvplot(
  fit, data = df,
  pval = TRUE,                             # show the log-rank test p-value
  conf.int = TRUE,                         # show confidence bands around the curves
  risk.table = TRUE,                       # table below the figure: number at risk over time
  xlab = "Days since index",               # axis labels
  ylab = "Event-free proportion"
)
```

With synthetic numbers the curves look like this (here the exposed drop faster, i.e. they get the outcome more often):

![Kaplan-Meier curves with synthetic data. `ggsurvplot()` typically also adds a risk table below the figure.](images/km_example.png)

::: {.callout-warning}
Kaplan-Meier can **overestimate the risk** if a competing event (typically death) can preclude your outcome - see [Competing risks](#konkurrerende-risici) below.
:::

## Cox regression (hazard ratio)

`coxph()` gives hazard ratios (HR) adjusted for covariates. `tbl_regression(exponentiate = TRUE)` shows them as HRs instead of log-hazards.

```r
library(gtsummary)                         # tbl_regression()

# Without replacement (each person appears only once): an ordinary Cox model.
cox <- coxph(Surv(time_days, outcome) ~ exposure + age + sex,
             data = df)

cox %>%                                    # pass the model on to a clean table
  tbl_regression(exponentiate = TRUE)      # exponentiate = TRUE -> hazard ratios
```

A hazard ratio reads like an OR/RR: **1 = no difference**, above 1 = higher rate, below 1 = lower. See [Reading your result](13d_regression.qmd#laes-resultat).

**The difference with replacement.** If the comparison cohort is matched **with replacement** (or there is crossover), the same person appears several times and the rows are not independent. Then you add **one single argument** - `cluster = pnr` - to exactly the same model. It does **not** change the hazard ratio estimate (the HR is the same); it only corrects the **standard errors and confidence intervals**, which would otherwise be artificially narrow. Same idea as in [Regression](13d_regression.qmd#klyngebaserede-standardfejl).

```r
# With replacement (same person several times): the ONLY difference from the model above is cluster = pnr.
coxph(Surv(time_days, outcome) ~ exposure + age + sex,
      data    = df,
      cluster = pnr) %>%                   # <- the only change: cluster on person id
  tbl_regression(exponentiate = TRUE)      # HR unchanged; only the CIs become correct (usually wider)
```

## Check the assumption: proportional hazards

The Cox model assumes **proportional hazards** (PH): that a variable's effect is constant over time. If it does not hold, the single hazard ratio is misleading. Always check it with `cox.zph()`, which is based on *Schoenfeld residuals* (in short, a measure of whether the effect "drifts" over time).

```r
ph <- cox.zph(cox)                         # formal PH test per variable + global
print(ph)                                  # small p-value = sign the assumption is VIOLATED
plot(ph)                                   # residual curve per variable; a sloping curve
                                           #   indicates a non-proportional hazard
```

<details>
<summary>What to do if the assumption is violated</summary>

If a variable violates the assumption, you can e.g. **stratify** on it (`+ strata(variable)` in the model, giving it its own baseline hazard) or **model a time-dependent effect** (let the effect change over follow-up).

</details>

## Competing risks {#konkurrerende-risici}

::: {.callout-warning}
If another event can **preclude** your outcome (classically: **death**, before the outcome can occur), it is a *competing risk*. A plain Kaplan-Meier then **overestimates** the risk, because it treats those who died as merely censored. This is the rule rather than the exception in register cohorts with older people or long follow-up.
:::

Use the **cumulative incidence function** (CIF, also called Aalen-Johansen) to describe the risk over time instead - it allocates people correctly across the competing outcomes.

```r
library(tidycmprsk)                        # cuminc(), crr()

# The status variable must be a FACTOR whose 1st level is "censored":
df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    status = factor(
      status_code,                         # e.g. 0 = censored, 1 = outcome, 2 = death
      levels = c(0, 1, 2),
      labels = c("censored", "outcome", "death")
    )
  )

# Cumulative incidence per group (+ Gray's test for a group difference):
cuminc(Surv(time_days, status) ~ exposure, data = df)
```

**Which model? It depends on your question.** There are two kinds of hazard, answering different things:

- **Cause-specific hazard:** run an ordinary Cox model where the competing event is **censored**. Answers the **etiologic** question - what affects the *rate* of the outcome itself?
- **Subdistribution hazard** (*Fine-Gray*): the competing events stay in the risk set instead of being censored. Answers the **prognostic** question - how is the *absolute, cumulative risk* affected?

```r
# Fine-Gray regression (subdistribution hazard ratio) - for absolute/cumulative risk:
crr(Surv(time_days, status) ~ exposure + age + sex, data = df)

# Cause-specific Cox - for etiology: censor the competing event (keep status == "outcome"):
coxph(Surv(time_days, status == "outcome") ~ exposure + age + sex, data = df)
```

<details>
<summary>Rule of thumb: which one do I pick?</summary>

If you want to **describe or predict risk** (e.g. "what is the 5-year risk of the outcome?"), use CIF + Fine-Gray. If you want to understand a **causal relationship** (e.g. "does the exposure raise the rate of the outcome?"), use the cause-specific Cox. Many studies report both.

</details>

## Time-varying covariates (briefly)

If a variable changes during follow-up (e.g. a blood test or BMI), it cannot be a single value per person. It must be in **start/stop format** (one row per interval), built with `survival::tmerge()` and analysed with `coxph(Surv(tstart, tstop, outcome) ~ ...)`. The reshaping itself is in [Time-varying variables](12d_tidsvarierende.qmd).

::: {.callout-note}
Remember: anything leaving DST must go through **output control** - no small cells, only aggregated results. See [Phase 14 - Export and repatriation](14_eksport-hjemsendelse.qmd).
:::
